_status: SIGNED-OFF 2026-07-13 — owner-authorized go-live (3rd batch). Class I-II (easy family float; occasional Class II rock-garden rapids, with an isolated Class III hole/wave train on the long lower run ~15 mi below Glenwood at high water)
name: Caddo River
difficultyRating: Class I-II (easy family float; occasional Class II rock-garden rapids, with an isolated Class III hole/wave train on the long lower run ~15 mi below Glenwood at high water)
description: A clear, spring-fed Ouachita Mountain stream and one of Arkansas's most popular family floats, running east past the boulder gorge at Caddo Gap toward DeGray Lake. The classic Caddo Gap-to-Glenwood run mixes fun Class I-II rock gardens with long, calm pools.
managingAuthority: Ouachita National Forest — Caddo-Womble Ranger District (offices in Glenwood & Mount Ida) manages the upper reaches; U.S. Army Corps of Engineers manages DeGray Lake at the lower end; the river corridor is otherwise predominantly private land. Arkansas Game & Fish / Arkansas Dept. of Agriculture involved in fisheries and gauge cooperation.
accessLaw: Arkansas navigable-waters doctrine: the public may float and use navigable streams and wade/portage up to the ordinary high-water mark. Navigability includes recreational use (Arkansas Supreme Court, post-1980) and a stream need only be navigable by small boat roughly six months per year to qualify; the Caddo is a recognized public float stream. However, the streambed banks are largely private and the public may NOT cross private land to reach the river — use established public/outfitter accesses.

gauges.name: Caddo River near Caddo Gap, AR

sections.name: Caddo Gap to Glenwood (the Classic Run)

putIn.name: Caddo Gap — Hwy 8 river access / outfitter accesses near Arrowhead (Montgomery County)

takeOut.name: near Amity — Hwy 182, ~1 mi north of Amity, toward the headwaters of DeGray Lake (Clark County)

research.calibrationKeySource: Caddo River Camping & Canoe Rental published gauge-height key (caddoriver.com), corroborated by American Whitewater + AR trip reports
research.calibrationNotes: UNIT = gauge height in FEET (USGS 07359610 param 00065), matching the outfitter key; the gauge also reports discharge (00060). Added a 'low' anchor is folded between too_low and optimal_min: caddoriver.com lists 5.25–5.4 ft as the low/family band ('some rocks to navigate around, ideal for inexperienced boaters and families, 4–6 hr'), so treat ~5.4 ft as the low-water/beginner level (confidence high, source caddoriver.com). cfs CROSS-REFERENCE (secondary): outfitter says great paddling up to ~650 cfs, swift-water skill needed >650 cfs, and advanced/expert-only >1,200 cfs. American Whitewater rating points: ~150 cfs≈5.7 ft (bony), ~500–600 cfs≈6.7 ft (swift Class II–III). rivers.moherp.org algorithmic (statistical, NOT authoritative) cfs bands: <50 too low, 50–109 low, ~174–350 good, 350–953 high, 953+ flood. Today (2026-07-13) the gauge reads ~4.93–4.95 ft / ~42–45 cfs — i.e. currently BELOW floatable, which independently validates the ~5.25 ft too_low anchor. USGS note on this gauge: 'the channel has moved to its original location; the datum has been adjusted for this change' — historical ft correlations may drift slightly from the current rating. FLOOD STAGE: the USGS legacy page lists NO NWS flood stage for 07359610 and there is no AHPS/NWPS forecast point at Caddo Gap (nearest is Caddo River at DeGray Dam, NWS id DGDA4); a '26.0 ft flood stage' surfaced in one aggregator search but is inconsistent with this gauge's ~5 ft normal datum and could not be verified for 07359610 — do NOT use it. Drainage area at the gauge ≈136 sq mi; median flow ≈65 cfs.
hazards: Flash flooding — this is the #1 danger. The Caddo is a flashy Ouachita Mountain river that can rise several feet very fast during/after heavy rain; the same region's June 2010 Albert Pike flash flood (nearby Little Missouri River) killed ~20 campers on gravel bars. Never camp low on gravel bars, and get off the water when the gauge is rising or storms are upstream.

Rapidly rising / high water: >650 cfs requires swift-water skill (sit-on-top kayaks safer than open canoes); >1,200 cfs (roughly >7 ft) is advanced/expert-only with tricky currents and strainers — do not take children or inexperienced paddlers.
Strainers / downed trees & willow thickets, especially at higher flows and on outside bends.

Low-water bridges / crossings (including the old low-water bridge near Caddo Gap) — submerged and dangerous at higher flows; thermal springs bubble up in the riverbed ~200 yds NW of the old low-water bridge.
Bridge by Arrowhead at Caddo Gap: the river narrows and the current runs fast under the bridge (pin/strainer risk).
Caddo Gap gorge: narrow, boulder-strewn novaculite/sandstone rapids and 'water gaps'; upper reaches average a steep ~29 ft/mi gradient (rockier, more technical than the Glenwood run).
Class III hole with a wave train immediately after, ~15 mi below Glenwood on the long lower run, at higher flows (~500–600+ cfs) per American Whitewater; passage on either side is minimal.
Low water (late summer, below ~5.25 ft): shallow rock gardens and shoals cause dragging/portaging and foot-entrapment risk if wading.
NOTE: no rapid specifically named 'the falls' could be sourced for the Caddo; the notable named/located features above are what published sources support.
